one_line_takeaway: GU shows that steering only the last 1-2 transformer layers' prompt-conditioned hidden states toward a rank-4 PCA 'safe-behavior' subspace, using purely synthetic anchor-based prompts (no original training data), matches or beats gradient/preference-based LLM unlearning baselines on ToFU and UnlearnPII while producing lower membership-inference privacy risk and non-gibberish 'refusal/uncertain' outputs rather than blank or substituted content.
key_finding: On ToFU Forget-10 (Table 1), GU achieves E.S./F.R. of 0.172/0.06 on LLaMA3.2-1B and 0.114/0.058 on LLaMA2-7B, with M.U. of 0.577 (-0.021 vs original) and 0.598 (-0.025) respectively, and PrivLeak of -22.27 and -1.496 -- the closest-to-zero PrivLeak among all compared methods on LLaMA2-7B. Under matched MIA privacy-risk measurement (Fig. 3, |AUC-0.5|), GU and GA achieve the lowest privacy risk across both base models and all three MIA scores (Min-K, Reference, Zlib). On output characteristics (Table 2, LLaMA3.2-1B Forget-10), GU attains A.F.=0.765, E.M.=0.524 with 'Refusal/Uncertain' output type, versus GA's near-zero E.M. (0.000) but 'Blank'/gibberish outputs, and GradDiff/NPO/SimNPO/UN-DIAL/RMU's 'Substitute' behavior (unrelated-content outputs) at higher E.M. (0.515-0.646). In the matched source-free baseline comparison (Table 4, same synthetic data for all methods), GU substantially outperforms all baselines: E.S. 0.172 vs 0.513-0.701 for the gradient/preference/representation baselines (GA excepted, which reaches 0.112 E.S. but M.U. collapses to 0.231), demonstrating the geometric objective itself (not just the synthetic-data pipeline) drives the gains. Layer ablation (Table 3) shows editing early/middle layers barely reduces E.S. (~0.487-0.502 after 40 epochs) while editing the last one-two layers drops E.S. to ~0.17-0.18 in fewer epochs (22-27). PCA analysis (Fig. 5) shows a rank-4 subspace already explains 63% and 58% of variance on the two edited layers, rising to 98% at rank 16, supporting the low-rank safe-behavior assumption; k=4 is used as the default. Runtime on LLaMA2-7B: GU converges in 31.29 minutes (8 epochs), with GU-specific preprocessing (synthetic-data construction, safe-reference extraction, safe-subspace construction) totaling 14.3 minutes, comparable to GradDiff/SimNPO and faster than UN-DIAL (44.51 min).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Geometric Unlearning (GU), a representation-level LLM unlearning method that operates on prompt-conditioned hidden states without requiring access to the original training corpus. GU has three stages plus a stabilizer: (1) build a low-rank 'safe-behavior subspace' via rank-k PCA on hidden states from a small set of safe reference prompts (refusal/uncertain-style responses); (2) construct anchor-conditioned virtual prompts by inserting a target entity anchor into diverse synthetic contextual templates, and extract window-pooled hidden states at the anchor position; (3) align these target hidden states to the frozen safe-behavior subspace using two losses -- a centroid-pull loss (Lcent, pulls the mean-shifted state toward the safe-reference mean) and a fold-back confinement loss (Lfold, penalizes normalized out-of-subspace residual energy via a reflection operator Rℓ=2Pℓ-I); and (4) a retain-side stabilization component with a background-keep KL loss on non-window tokens of the unlearning prompts and a synthetic retain-distillation KL loss against a frozen teacher (same checkpoint as the student), to limit collateral drift on non-target content and lexically-confusable names. The full objective is Ltotal = Lcore (Lcent+Lfold) + Lretain (Lbg+Lret), applied at the last one or two transformer layers with PCA rank k=4.
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model in the classic attacker/defender sense; the paper frames a data-governance/privacy risk: standard gradient-based LLM unlearning pipelines require re-accessing the original (sensitive) target unlearning data Df and a large retain corpus Dr during the unlearning process itself, which the paper argues re-exposes sensitive data and creates an additional privacy exposure surface during a supposedly protective operation. The evaluation adopts membership inference attacks (MIAs) as a privacy audit of the unlearned model: three complementary MIA scoring rules (Min-K, Reference, Zlib) are used to try to distinguish forget-set (member) examples from non-member holdout examples via model-derived signals (AUC), with privacy risk quantified as deviation from chance, |AUC-0.5| (lower = better, i.e., unlearning that leaves no exploitable membership signal).
limitations: Limitations stated by the authors themselves in the Conclusion: GU's effectiveness depends on targets being reliably invoked by the chosen anchors and on synthetic prompts adequately covering real query contexts seen at deployment; performance may degrade for aliases, multilingual variants, or indirect mentions of the target entity, and distribution shift can leave rare contexts under-covered. GU aligns with standard extractability-based evaluation metrics but the authors explicitly state it does not certify complete representational erasure under stronger white-box audits. The sample-budget ablation (Fig. 4) shows a real trade-off: 40 synthetic samples strengthens unlearning further but at a 'noticeable utility cost' not fully offset by matched 1:1 retain supervision, and runtime grows monotonically with sample budget. On UnlearnPII (Fig. 6), GU does not always attain the strongest target-knowledge suppression achievable by the most aggressive baselines (e.g., GA, RMU), trading some suppression strength for higher retained utility. The Impact Statement is a boilerplate one-liner with no unlearning-specific risk discussion (e.g., dual-use of the anchor-injection/steering mechanism itself).

quantitative_results: Table 2 (LLaMA3.2-1B Forget-10): GU A.F.=0.765, E.M.=0.524, output type Refusal/Uncertain; Original A.F.=0.855, E.M.=0.974
Matched source-free comparison (Table 4, LLaMA3.2-1B): GU E.S.=0.172/F.R.=0.060/PrivLeak=-22.3/M.U.=0.577 vs baselines' E.S. range 0.112 (GA, but M.U. collapses to 0.231) to 0.701 (UN-DIAL)
Layer ablation (Table 3): last-two-layer editing E.S.=0.172, M.U.=0.577, converges in 27 epochs vs first-layer editing E.S.=0.498 after 40 epochs
PCA explained variance (Fig. 5): rank-4 subspace explains 63% and 58% of variance on the two edited layers of LLaMA2-7B; rank-16 explains 98% on both

Runtime (Table 5, LLaMA2-7B): GU total training 31.29 min over 8 epochs; GU-specific preprocessing = 12.37 min (virtual data construction) + 1.59 min (safe-reference extraction) + 0.34 min (safe-subspace construction)
